output_text:
Revised the section to resolve a load-bearing contradiction (retrieval is denied in the methods, then credited as the primary driver of results), tighten novelty claims, and pre-empt the obvious self-correction critique.

## Key problems in the original

1. **Direct contradiction.** ¶2 says "no external retrieval at any stage; the system relies entirely on the model's parametric knowledge." ¶4 says retrieval of IPCC AR6 excerpts is "the primary driver of the hallucination reduction." A reviewer will stop reading here. You must pick one.
2. **RAG dismissal is incoherent given the results.** Excluding RAG from the benchmark because it "addresses a different problem" is indefensible if your own pipeline does retrieval. Even if it didn't, RAG is the obvious baseline for factuality on a knowledge-intensive task.
3. **Novelty claim is brittle.** "First to apply iterative LLM self-feedback to climate narrative generation" is a domain-narrowed first; reviewers will read it as "Self-Refine (Madaan et al. 2023) applied to a new vertical." Either own that framing or articulate what climate narratives demand that generic self-refine doesn't.
4. **Self-critique mechanism is under-motivated.** Huang et al. (2024) and others have shown LLMs struggle to self-correct factual errors without external signal. If your critic is the same model with the same parametric knowledge, you owe the reader an argument for why this works — and the retrieval step (once you admit it) is exactly that argument.
5. **Evaluation specifics are missing.** "Expert-rated hallucination rate" — how many raters, agreement statistic, rubric, blinding? 50 municipalities — selection criteria? 12% → 4% — sample size and CI?
6. **Stopping criterion is fragile.** Jaccard <5% on entities/numerics will halt on cosmetic stability while substantive errors persist. At minimum acknowledge this; ideally add a content-level check.

## Revised version (assuming retrieval IS used — the empirical version)

> Climate impact narratives — region-specific risk summaries that municipal adaptation planners use to translate GCM-downscaled projections, sectoral vulnerability data, and policy context into actionable prose — demand both broad synthesis and quantitative fidelity. Single-pass LLM summarization, the dominant paradigm in current climate-NLP work, conflates these requirements and leaves quantitative claims unchecked. We propose **ICRN (Iterative Climate Refinement Narrator)**, which couples self-critique with targeted retrieval: one LLM drafts a regional narrative, then in each refinement loop the same model critiques the draft and issues retrieval queries against IPCC AR6 chapter text to verify quantitative claims before producing a revision.
>
> ICRN is, to our knowledge, the first system to combine iterative self-refinement with claim-conditional retrieval for climate narrative generation. The combination is not incidental: prior work on self-refinement (Madaan et al. 2023; Huang et al. 2024) shows that an LLM critic sharing parametric knowledge with its generator rarely surfaces novel factual errors. Retrieval provides the external signal that makes the critique loop productive; the iterative scaffold, in turn, lets retrieval be targeted at claims the critic flags as load-bearing rather than at the full draft.
>
> **Architecture.** A single base model plays both generator and critic. The critic prompt directs the model to flag (i) unsupported quantitative claims, (ii) missing impact categories from a fixed checklist (heat, flood, drought, vector-borne disease), and (iii) internal inconsistencies; flagged quantitative claims are rewritten as retrieval queries against an IPCC AR6 chapter index. The loop terminates when two consecutive drafts differ by less than 5% Jaccard similarity over named entities and numeric spans **and** no new claims are flagged by the critic — the second condition guards against cosmetic stability with residual errors.
>
> **Baselines.** We compare against single-pass generation, few-shot prompting, chain-of-thought, and standard RAG (retrieval applied to the initial draft only) using the same base model and corpus. The RAG comparison isolates the contribution of iteration over and above retrieval alone.
>
> **Results preview.** Across 50 mid-sized U.S. municipalities (selection criteria in §3.1), three expert raters scored narratives blind to condition; ICRN reduces hallucination rate from 12% (single-pass) to 4% (κ = 0.71). An ablation in §4 attributes roughly two-thirds of the gain to retrieval and the remainder to iteration; neither component alone is sufficient.

## Uncertainty disclosure

- The expert-rater details (3 raters, κ = 0.71) and the "two-thirds / one-third" attribution are placeholders illustrating what the section needs — replace with your actual numbers.
- The Huang et al. (2024) citation refers to the well-known "Large Language Models Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet" line of work; verify the exact citation you want to use.
- I assumed retrieval IS in your pipeline because the results paragraph credits it. If the truth is the opposite — no retrieval, and ¶4 was a stray draft fragment — the revision needs to go the other direction: drop the retrieval framing, add RAG as a baseline, and make a much more cautious claim about what self-critique alone buys you.
